romania.title: Federația Argentiniană de Fotbal, în vizorul FBI. Posibile fraude și spălări de bani în SUA
romania.summary: Condusă de superstarul internațional Lionel Messi, echipa de fotbal a Argentinei a ajuns în sferturile de finală ale Cupei Mondiale FIFA 2026, urmărind să-și apere titlul câștigat la Cupa Mondială din 2022. Cu toate acestea, pe lângă provocările de pe teren, se pare că echipa va trebui să facă față și unei bătălii juridice. Conform unor informații publicate de ziarul argentinian La Nación, Biroul Federal de Investigații (FBI) anchetează Federația Argentiniană de Fotbal (AFA) pentru posibile fraude și spălare de bani în Statele Unite, pe măsură ce echipa avansează în Cupa Mondială.

Părinții au fost șocați: „Ce să le zicem vecinilor? Că băiatul nostru lasă linii neterminate? Mai bine le ziceam că merge la teatru. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

medical.title: Learning to identify new objects reshapes parts of the brain, research finds
medical.summary: The wiring and rewiring of the brain never ends. Neural pathways are constantly being reshaped as we interact with the world and learn new things. At York University and MIT's McGovern Institute, scientists are combining detailed analysis of brain activity with computational modeling to better understand that change.

medical.title: Culturally tailored maternity care lifts healthy birth rates for First Nations families
medical.summary: The Baggarrook Yurrongi or "Women's Journey" model ensures women having a First Nations baby are cared for by a known midwife throughout pregnancy, birth and the early postnatal period.

medical.title: Epigenetic mapping provides deeper insight into leukemia
medical.summary: Researchers at Karolinska Institutet in Sweden and Kyoto University in Japan have identified new subgroups of the blood cancer acute myeloid leukemia. The study, published in the journal Nature, shows that changes in the regulation of genes within cells can help explain variation in the disease and influence prognosis and treatment choices.

medical.title: FDA expands use of first sickle cell disease gene therapy for children as young as 2 years
medical.summary: The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has issued a supplemental approval for Casgevy (exagamglogene autotemcel) for patients ages 2 and older with either sickle cell disease (SCD) with recurrent vaso-occlusive crises (VOCs) or transfusion-dependent β thalassemia (TDT). Supplemental approval was granted to Vertex Pharmaceuticals.

medical.title: Psychedelic drug screen in mice may overlook stress and brain changes
medical.summary: Over the past decades, some medical researchers and neuroscientists have been exploring the possible therapeutic effects of psychedelic compounds, such as lysergic acid diethylamide (LSD) and psilocybin. These are substances that act on serotonin receptors in the brain and can alter people's perceptions, mood and mental processes. Some preliminary studies suggest that they could help treat specific psychiatric conditions, such as major depression and post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD). Nonetheless, psychedelics also prompt hallucinations, distorted perceptions and heightened emotions, which can be distressing and unfavorable for many psychiatric patients.

medical.title: Researchers expand plasmonic liquid biopsy platform to early-stage colorectal cancer
medical.summary: A research team led by Minyoung Lee and Sunggyu Park of The Korea Institute of Materials Science (KIMS) Bio & Health Materials Research Division has developed a plasmonic-based liquid biopsy platform capable of ultrasensitive detection of KRAS mutations in the blood and urine of patients with early-stage colorectal cancer.

medical.title: Researchers uncover hormone link between gut and brain in anorexia nervosa
medical.summary: People with anorexia nervosa have unusually high levels of a hormone called LEAP2 in their blood when they are in the acute phase of the disorder, according to research presented at the Federation of European Neuroscience Societies (FENS) Forum 2026. Patients with the highest levels of LEAP2 are more likely to relapse following treatment for anorexia nervosa.

medical.title: GLP-1 agonists may help people with diabetes and long COVID, study in mice suggests
medical.summary: GLP-1 agonists have become popular treatments for weight loss and type 2 diabetes, and newer studies suggest other health benefits as well. Findings from experiments on mice, published in the Journal of Virology, suggest that the drugs may offer benefits to another patient population: people with type 2 diabetes who suffer from pulmonary fibrosis months or years after infection with SARS-CoV-2, the virus that causes COVID-19.

medical.title: Genetic study links IBS to lipid metabolism and triglyceride regulation
medical.summary: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) is a common condition that affects more than 10% of the general population, causing recurrent abdominal pain, bloating, constipation and diarrhea. IBS is considered a disorder of gut-brain interaction, and previous research, including genetic research, has established links to nerve signaling, the nervous system and other conditions including anxiety and mood disorders. The exact biological mechanisms, however, have remained frustratingly difficult to pin down, leaving many patients without effective, targeted treatments.

medical.title: Huntington's treatment quest reaches first pluripotent stem cell trial, with safety now under review
medical.summary: An update on the first clinical trial evaluating a pluripotent stem cell-derived neural stem cell therapy for Huntington's disease was presented at the ISSCR 2026 Annual Meeting. The Phase Ib/IIa REGEN4HD study represents a significant translational milestone following years of preclinical research, pivotal safety studies, regulatory engagement and clinical trial development.

medical.title: Data demonstrate 1-year survival of stem cell-derived neural progenitor cells in patients with retinitis pigmentosa
medical.summary: New, previously unpublished clinical data have been presented at ISSCR 2026 demonstrating that transplanted human neural progenitor cells survived for at least one year following subretinal transplantation in patients with retinitis pigmentosa (RP), while maintaining a favorable safety profile.

medical.title: New clinical data advances stem cell-based cell therapy for Parkinson's disease
medical.summary: New clinical data from the STEM-PD Phase I/II clinical trial have been presented at the ISSCR 2026 Annual Meeting. The study reports 12-month outcomes evaluating a cryopreserved, off-the-shelf dopaminergic progenitor cell product derived from human pluripotent stem cells for the treatment of Parkinson's disease.

medical.title: Long-term stroke data reveal stem cell therapy's safety, biological activity over years
medical.summary: Previously unpublished long-term clinical and imaging findings from participants treated with an induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived neural progenitor cell therapy for chronic ischemic stroke were presented at the ISSCR 2026 Annual Meeting.

medical.title: New data highlight off-the-shelf iPSC-derived CAR T-cell therapy for treatment-resistant systemic sclerosis
medical.summary: New preliminary clinical data presented at ISSCR 2026 evaluated an induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived CAR T-cell therapy for patients with treatment-resistant systemic sclerosis, a rare autoimmune disease with high mortality.

medical.title: Medical AI may look less biased on paper but not in practice, new study finds
medical.summary: Large language models (LLMs) are only as good as the data they learn from. If their training data contains social biases, the models may unintentionally repeat those biases in their responses. As their use increases with the rise of generative AI, it has become evident that they contain biases that reinforce stereotypes based on factors like ethnicity, gender or geography. This is concerning because LLM-based chatbots have become an integral part of the health care system, where health-related stigma can negatively affect one's diagnosis and care.

medical.title: Does a person's neighborhood impact their risk of pancreatic cancer?
medical.summary: A new Yale-led study published in JNCI Cancer Spectrum is the first to examine the overall relationship between pancreatic cancer and neighborhood-level socioeconomic factors.

medical.title: ADHD medication misuse declines among US adults, study finds
medical.summary: Misuse of stimulant medications for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) has declined sharply. In 2023, approximately 3.7% of adults younger than 30 misused these medications, down from 7.5% in 2016. In 2023, 1.9% of adults of all ages misused them, with misuse more common among younger adults, white adults, people living in cities and college students. The results were published in the Journal of Clinical Psychopharmacology.

medical.title: Eight-patient Parkinson's stem cell clinical trial confirms feasibility
medical.summary: A landmark study led by Lund, Sweden, has shown that transplanting stem cell-derived dopamine progenitor cells into the brain is feasible. Eight patients were transplanted in the first clinical trial, and no serious side effects linked to the transplanted cells were seen during the first year of follow-up. The results are published in Nature Medicine.

science.title: Wearables to track plant health: Farmers could use real-time information to manage crop conditions
science.summary: A smartwatch can tell us the level of oxygen in our blood, when our sleep is restless or the number of steps we take in a day. Now imagine that kind of tracking ability for plants. By the time farmers see curling leaves or stunted growth in their fields, their crops may already have spent days under stress.

science.title: Sustainability reporting no longer shields companies from criticism
science.summary: In the past few years, new rules from governments across Europe have required companies to increase their reporting on their sustainability efforts. Since then, stakeholders such as NGOs and journalists have been able to use those insights to ramp up their criticism of companies. That's according to a new study published this month in the Journal of Management Studies.

science.title: Genetic crossovers defy chromosome-length model in male and female mice
science.summary: A Cornell-led study is challenging a decades-old explanation for how chromosomes exchange genetic material within the biological process that forms eggs and sperm in mammals.

science.title: Shackleton's final ship is no longer just a sonar shadow
science.summary: An expedition led by the Royal Canadian Geographical Society in partnership with Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution has obtained the first close-up images of the wreck of Quest, the last ship of famed Antarctic explorer Sir Ernest Shackleton, in the Labrador Sea. The images were obtained by WHOI's Falcon ROV and DSV Alvin, the first submersible to visit the wreck of Titanic 40 years ago.

science.title: What if our homes could move?
science.summary: Imagine living in a home that you could simply pick up and move when extreme weather strikes. Instead of bricks and mortar, it's made from materials sourced from the local environment and, if weather conditions change, you pack it up and rebuild it in a safer location. That's exactly what Khudi Bari, developed by Bangladeshi firm Marina Tabassum Architects, is doing.

science.title: AI can predict how you'll respond to a survey—but that's not the same as understanding you
science.summary: What makes people change their minds or their behavior? Social scientists spend a lot of time thinking about this question, and experiments are one of the most powerful ways to answer it.

science.title: Study identifies key mechanism regulating how cells use fat to generate energy
science.summary: An international study by scientists at the Centro Nacional de Investigaciones Cardiovasculares Carlos III (CNIC) and the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) has identified a fundamental mechanism that regulates how the body uses stored fat to produce energy.

science.title: Transparent nanosheets could shrink phone cameras while preserving high-resolution color images
science.summary: Researchers at Nagoya University in Japan have developed gallium-doped zinc oxide (GZO) nanosheets that may enhance camera resolution in compact devices, including smartphones and medical endoscopes.

science.title: STING protein: Study finds new ways for the body to activate and possibly control inflammation
science.summary: Understanding inflammation—and, above all, how to regulate it—is one of the great medical challenges of modern medicine. Its role as the first line of defense is crucial. It occurs when the presence of infectious agents triggers an inflammatory response. As well as hindering the entry of viruses and bacteria, this acts as a distress signal, attracting other components of our immune system.

science.title: 'Check your ingredients': A new blueprint for using Fermi's 'Golden Rule'
science.summary: Underpinning much of modern technology, from smartphones to scanning tunneling microscopes to particle colliders, is Fermi's Golden Rule. Named for 20th-century Italian American physicist Enrico Fermi (but actually discovered by British physicist Paul Dirac), the rule is a formula that connects what can be measured in an experiment—such as how fast atoms "jump" between energy states—to the microscopic properties of a quantum mechanical system. The formula is taught in every undergraduate quantum physics class.

science.title: The untapped potential of bowel cancer samples to boost understanding of other diseases
science.summary: About half a million samples are collected from over-50s in Scotland each year in a highly successful NHS program that significantly boosts early cancer detection. But only a tiny amount of the sent-in poo—mixed with fluid—is needed to test for traces of blood, and the rest is discarded.

science.title: A new route to electrically controlled helimagnetic structures
science.summary: Advanced magnetic memory and spintronic devices rely on the ability to control magnetic states using electricity. Today, such technologies work by manipulating relatively simple magnetic structures found in ferromagnets, where all the magnetic moments point the same way. However, researchers are becoming increasingly interested in controlling more complex magnetic systems because these could offer higher information density and improved efficiency.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

Cu toate acestea, pe lângă provocările de pe teren, se pare că echipa va trebui să facă față și unei bătălii juridice. Conform unor informații publicate de ziarul argentinian La Nación, Biroul Federal de Investigații (FBI) anchetează Federația Argentiniană de Fotbal (AFA) pentru posibile fraude și spălare de bani în Statele Unite, pe măsură ce echipa avansează în Cupa Mondială.

mix_items.summary: Conferința naţională RCEPB şi Caravana OAER / UTCB / AIIR va avea loc între 11-13 septembrie 2026 la Băile Tușnad. În perioada 11-13 septembrie 2026, la Băile Tușnad, va avea… Articolul Conferința RCEPB – cel mai important eveniment național dedicat promovării performanței energetice a clădirilor, decarbonizării mediului construit și implementării politicilor europene de sustenabilitate și circularitate în sectorul clădirilor apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: 650 de profesori și peste 16.900 de elevi au explorat inteligența artificială, siguranța online, robotica și tehnologiile viitorului prin 31 de activități educaționale digitale disponibile prin platforma Școala din viitor… Articolul Peste 16.900 de elevi și 650 de profesori au beneficiat de experiențe de învățare digitală în anul școlar 2025-2026, prin proiectul Școala din viitor ajunge în școlile JA apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Un tânăr din România nu a mai fost lăsat de părinți la Beach, Please!, după ce anul trecut a comis rușinea familiei: n-a tras toată linia din farfurie. Părinții au fost șocați: „Ce să le zicem vecinilor? Că băiatul nostru lasă linii neterminate? Mai bine le ziceam că merge la teatru. Privind în urmă, semnele […] The post Un tânăr n-a fost lăsat la Beach, Please! de părinți, că anul trecut n-a tras toată linia din farfurie appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: Universitatea „Constantin Brâncuși” din Târgu Jiu organizează, în perioada 28–30 august 2026, UCB Summer Camp 2026, un proiect educațional dedicat viitorilor studenți ai universității, care au obținut cele mai bune… Articolul Universitatea „Constantin Brâncuși” din Târgu Jiu organizează UCB Summer Camp 2026, dedicată viitorilor studenți ai universității apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

Potrivit unei hotărâri de anul trecut a Consiliului Local Costineşti, cântăreţii de rap nu mai au voie să participe la festivaluri pe teritoriul comunei decât însoţiţi de […] The post De când Wiz Khalifa a fumat pe scenă, rapperii americani au voie la Beach Please doar cu părinţii appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

mix_items.title: Learning to identify new objects reshapes parts of the brain, research finds
mix_items.summary: The wiring and rewiring of the brain never ends. Neural pathways are constantly being reshaped as we interact with the world and learn new things. At York University and MIT's McGovern Institute, scientists are combining detailed analysis of brain activity with computational modeling to better understand that change.

mix_items.title: Wearables to track plant health: Farmers could use real-time information to manage crop conditions
mix_items.summary: A smartwatch can tell us the level of oxygen in our blood, when our sleep is restless or the number of steps we take in a day. Now imagine that kind of tracking ability for plants. By the time farmers see curling leaves or stunted growth in their fields, their crops may already have spent days under stress.

mix_items.title: Culturally tailored maternity care lifts healthy birth rates for First Nations families
mix_items.summary: The Baggarrook Yurrongi or "Women's Journey" model ensures women having a First Nations baby are cared for by a known midwife throughout pregnancy, birth and the early postnatal period.

mix_items.title: Sustainability reporting no longer shields companies from criticism
mix_items.summary: In the past few years, new rules from governments across Europe have required companies to increase their reporting on their sustainability efforts. Since then, stakeholders such as NGOs and journalists have been able to use those insights to ramp up their criticism of companies. That's according to a new study published this month in the Journal of Management Studies.

mix_items.title: Epigenetic mapping provides deeper insight into leukemia
mix_items.summary: Researchers at Karolinska Institutet in Sweden and Kyoto University in Japan have identified new subgroups of the blood cancer acute myeloid leukemia. The study, published in the journal Nature, shows that changes in the regulation of genes within cells can help explain variation in the disease and influence prognosis and treatment choices.

mix_items.title: Genetic crossovers defy chromosome-length model in male and female mice
mix_items.summary: A Cornell-led study is challenging a decades-old explanation for how chromosomes exchange genetic material within the biological process that forms eggs and sperm in mammals.

mix_items.title: FDA expands use of first sickle cell disease gene therapy for children as young as 2 years
mix_items.summary: The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has issued a supplemental approval for Casgevy (exagamglogene autotemcel) for patients ages 2 and older with either sickle cell disease (SCD) with recurrent vaso-occlusive crises (VOCs) or transfusion-dependent β thalassemia (TDT). Supplemental approval was granted to Vertex Pharmaceuticals.

mix_items.title: Shackleton's final ship is no longer just a sonar shadow
mix_items.summary: An expedition led by the Royal Canadian Geographical Society in partnership with Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution has obtained the first close-up images of the wreck of Quest, the last ship of famed Antarctic explorer Sir Ernest Shackleton, in the Labrador Sea. The images were obtained by WHOI's Falcon ROV and DSV Alvin, the first submersible to visit the wreck of Titanic 40 years ago.

mix_items.title: What if our homes could move?
mix_items.summary: Imagine living in a home that you could simply pick up and move when extreme weather strikes. Instead of bricks and mortar, it's made from materials sourced from the local environment and, if weather conditions change, you pack it up and rebuild it in a safer location. That's exactly what Khudi Bari, developed by Bangladeshi firm Marina Tabassum Architects, is doing.

mix_items.title: Psychedelic drug screen in mice may overlook stress and brain changes
mix_items.summary: Over the past decades, some medical researchers and neuroscientists have been exploring the possible therapeutic effects of psychedelic compounds, such as lysergic acid diethylamide (LSD) and psilocybin. These are substances that act on serotonin receptors in the brain and can alter people's perceptions, mood and mental processes. Some preliminary studies suggest that they could help treat specific psychiatric conditions, such as major depression and post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD). Nonetheless, psychedelics also prompt hallucinations, distorted perceptions and heightened emotions, which can be distressing and unfavorable for many psychiatric patients.

mix_items.title: Researchers expand plasmonic liquid biopsy platform to early-stage colorectal cancer
mix_items.summary: A research team led by Minyoung Lee and Sunggyu Park of The Korea Institute of Materials Science (KIMS) Bio & Health Materials Research Division has developed a plasmonic-based liquid biopsy platform capable of ultrasensitive detection of KRAS mutations in the blood and urine of patients with early-stage colorectal cancer.

mix_items.title: AI can predict how you'll respond to a survey—but that's not the same as understanding you
mix_items.summary: What makes people change their minds or their behavior? Social scientists spend a lot of time thinking about this question, and experiments are one of the most powerful ways to answer it.

mix_items.title: Researchers uncover hormone link between gut and brain in anorexia nervosa
mix_items.summary: People with anorexia nervosa have unusually high levels of a hormone called LEAP2 in their blood when they are in the acute phase of the disorder, according to research presented at the Federation of European Neuroscience Societies (FENS) Forum 2026. Patients with the highest levels of LEAP2 are more likely to relapse following treatment for anorexia nervosa.

mix_items.title: Study identifies key mechanism regulating how cells use fat to generate energy
mix_items.summary: An international study by scientists at the Centro Nacional de Investigaciones Cardiovasculares Carlos III (CNIC) and the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) has identified a fundamental mechanism that regulates how the body uses stored fat to produce energy.

mix_items.title: GLP-1 agonists may help people with diabetes and long COVID, study in mice suggests
mix_items.summary: GLP-1 agonists have become popular treatments for weight loss and type 2 diabetes, and newer studies suggest other health benefits as well. Findings from experiments on mice, published in the Journal of Virology, suggest that the drugs may offer benefits to another patient population: people with type 2 diabetes who suffer from pulmonary fibrosis months or years after infection with SARS-CoV-2, the virus that causes COVID-19.

mix_items.title: Genetic study links IBS to lipid metabolism and triglyceride regulation
mix_items.summary: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) is a common condition that affects more than 10% of the general population, causing recurrent abdominal pain, bloating, constipation and diarrhea. IBS is considered a disorder of gut-brain interaction, and previous research, including genetic research, has established links to nerve signaling, the nervous system and other conditions including anxiety and mood disorders. The exact biological mechanisms, however, have remained frustratingly difficult to pin down, leaving many patients without effective, targeted treatments.

mix_items.title: Transparent nanosheets could shrink phone cameras while preserving high-resolution color images
mix_items.summary: Researchers at Nagoya University in Japan have developed gallium-doped zinc oxide (GZO) nanosheets that may enhance camera resolution in compact devices, including smartphones and medical endoscopes.

mix_items.title: Huntington's treatment quest reaches first pluripotent stem cell trial, with safety now under review
mix_items.summary: An update on the first clinical trial evaluating a pluripotent stem cell-derived neural stem cell therapy for Huntington's disease was presented at the ISSCR 2026 Annual Meeting. The Phase Ib/IIa REGEN4HD study represents a significant translational milestone following years of preclinical research, pivotal safety studies, regulatory engagement and clinical trial development.

mix_items.title: Data demonstrate 1-year survival of stem cell-derived neural progenitor cells in patients with retinitis pigmentosa
mix_items.summary: New, previously unpublished clinical data have been presented at ISSCR 2026 demonstrating that transplanted human neural progenitor cells survived for at least one year following subretinal transplantation in patients with retinitis pigmentosa (RP), while maintaining a favorable safety profile.

mix_items.title: New clinical data advances stem cell-based cell therapy for Parkinson's disease
mix_items.summary: New clinical data from the STEM-PD Phase I/II clinical trial have been presented at the ISSCR 2026 Annual Meeting. The study reports 12-month outcomes evaluating a cryopreserved, off-the-shelf dopaminergic progenitor cell product derived from human pluripotent stem cells for the treatment of Parkinson's disease.

mix_items.title: Long-term stroke data reveal stem cell therapy's safety, biological activity over years
mix_items.summary: Previously unpublished long-term clinical and imaging findings from participants treated with an induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived neural progenitor cell therapy for chronic ischemic stroke were presented at the ISSCR 2026 Annual Meeting.

mix_items.title: New data highlight off-the-shelf iPSC-derived CAR T-cell therapy for treatment-resistant systemic sclerosis
mix_items.summary: New preliminary clinical data presented at ISSCR 2026 evaluated an induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived CAR T-cell therapy for patients with treatment-resistant systemic sclerosis, a rare autoimmune disease with high mortality.

mix_items.title: STING protein: Study finds new ways for the body to activate and possibly control inflammation
mix_items.summary: Understanding inflammation—and, above all, how to regulate it—is one of the great medical challenges of modern medicine. Its role as the first line of defense is crucial. It occurs when the presence of infectious agents triggers an inflammatory response. As well as hindering the entry of viruses and bacteria, this acts as a distress signal, attracting other components of our immune system.

mix_items.title: Medical AI may look less biased on paper but not in practice, new study finds
mix_items.summary: Large language models (LLMs) are only as good as the data they learn from. If their training data contains social biases, the models may unintentionally repeat those biases in their responses. As their use increases with the rise of generative AI, it has become evident that they contain biases that reinforce stereotypes based on factors like ethnicity, gender or geography. This is concerning because LLM-based chatbots have become an integral part of the health care system, where health-related stigma can negatively affect one's diagnosis and care.

mix_items.title: 'Check your ingredients': A new blueprint for using Fermi's 'Golden Rule'
mix_items.summary: Underpinning much of modern technology, from smartphones to scanning tunneling microscopes to particle colliders, is Fermi's Golden Rule. Named for 20th-century Italian American physicist Enrico Fermi (but actually discovered by British physicist Paul Dirac), the rule is a formula that connects what can be measured in an experiment—such as how fast atoms "jump" between energy states—to the microscopic properties of a quantum mechanical system. The formula is taught in every undergraduate quantum physics class.

mix_items.title: Does a person's neighborhood impact their risk of pancreatic cancer?
mix_items.summary: A new Yale-led study published in JNCI Cancer Spectrum is the first to examine the overall relationship between pancreatic cancer and neighborhood-level socioeconomic factors.

mix_items.title: The untapped potential of bowel cancer samples to boost understanding of other diseases
mix_items.summary: About half a million samples are collected from over-50s in Scotland each year in a highly successful NHS program that significantly boosts early cancer detection. But only a tiny amount of the sent-in poo—mixed with fluid—is needed to test for traces of blood, and the rest is discarded.

mix_items.title: ADHD medication misuse declines among US adults, study finds
mix_items.summary: Misuse of stimulant medications for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) has declined sharply. In 2023, approximately 3.7% of adults younger than 30 misused these medications, down from 7.5% in 2016. In 2023, 1.9% of adults of all ages misused them, with misuse more common among younger adults, white adults, people living in cities and college students. The results were published in the Journal of Clinical Psychopharmacology.

mix_items.title: Eight-patient Parkinson's stem cell clinical trial confirms feasibility
mix_items.summary: A landmark study led by Lund, Sweden, has shown that transplanting stem cell-derived dopamine progenitor cells into the brain is feasible. Eight patients were transplanted in the first clinical trial, and no serious side effects linked to the transplanted cells were seen during the first year of follow-up. The results are published in Nature Medicine.

mix_items.title: A new route to electrically controlled helimagnetic structures
mix_items.summary: Advanced magnetic memory and spintronic devices rely on the ability to control magnetic states using electricity. Today, such technologies work by manipulating relatively simple magnetic structures found in ferromagnets, where all the magnetic moments point the same way. However, researchers are becoming increasingly interested in controlling more complex magnetic systems because these could offer higher information density and improved efficiency.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Ce faci diseara? - Ma odihnesc de la cat n-am facut azi.

satire_ro.title: Pistoale cadou de la Erdogan pentru toţi liderii NATO. Nicuşor Dan a primit unul cu apă
